images.caption: Figure 1:Performance overview of LeapAlign.(a) Comparison of reward improvement during fine-tuning on the compositional alignment task. LeapAlign achieves faster and higher reward gains than DRTune[52]. (b) LeapAlign consistently improves Flux across multiple evaluators. (c) LeapAlign shows clear gains on the GenEval benchmark. For clearer visualization of performance gains, we shift the radar chart origin to 60% of the FLUX.1-Dev performance and set the maximum radius to the best performance among the displayed methods.

Figure 2:Overview of LeapAlign.x1,…,x0x_{1},...,x_{0}are the latents in the full generation trajectory, wherex1x_{1}andx0x_{0}correspond to noise and the clean image, respectively. Our method builds twoleaps: fromxkx_{k}we predictx^j∣k\hat{x}_{j\mid k}using the velocity predicted atxkx_{k}, and fromxjx_{j}we predictx^0∣j\hat{x}_{0\mid j}using the velocity predicted atxjx_{j}. Here, all latents and velocity predictions are obtained during online sampling. We also compute thelatent connectorto connect the real latent and its one-step approximation. The twoleapsand the twolatent connectorsform atwo-step leap trajectory222The latent connectors,e.g., fromx^j∣k\hat{x}_{j\mid k}toxjx_{j}, are not counted as a step because they do not involve prediction using the flow matching model.. It is along the leap trajectory instead of the full trajectory that reward gradient canflowefficiently. Further, becausekkandjjare randomly selected, ultimately LeapAlign can update any generation step.

Figure 3:Qualitative comparison on GenEval benchmark. We compare direct-gradient post-training methods and the base model Flux. These examples show our method can generate high-quality images aligned with text prompts.[⋅,⋅][\,\cdot,\cdot\,]indicates the timestep range used for training.
(a)Effectiveness of gradient discounting.We vary the value ofα\alpha(Eq.10) and evaluate LeapAlign. Settingα=0.3\alpha=0.3yields the best performance.
(a)Effectiveness of gradient discounting.We vary the value ofα\alpha(Eq.10) and evaluate LeapAlign. Settingα=0.3\alpha=0.3yields the best performance.
(b)Comparing using one, two, and three steps in leap trajectories.Using two steps results in the best trade-off between performance and memory cost.
(c)Comparison of different inputs of the reward model.‘x^0∣j+d0\hat{x}_{0\mid j}+d_{0}’ computes trajectory-similarity weighting withdjd_{j}andd0d_{0}. We can see that usingx0x_{0}is superior.

(d)Comparing trajectory similarity weighting methods.‘djd_{j}andd0d_{0}’: our method (Eq.12). ‘djd_{j}’ and ‘d0d_{0}’ only measure trajectory differences atxjx_{j}andx0x_{0}, respectively. ‘w/o’: this mechanism is not applied. Our method has the best result.

(e)Impact of training timestep range.We construct leap trajectories by randomly selecting timesteps from different ranges, where11is the earliest timestep. Random selection over the full timestep range[0,1][0,1]has better performance.

(f)Comparing strategies for selectingkkandjj.‘Fixed(k,j)(k,j)Distance’: fixing the distance betweenkkandjjto1/21/2. ‘Random’ meanskkandjjare randomly selected (Section4.2). Random selection performs better and is easier to implement.

header: 9Visualization of GenEval Score Improvement During Fine-Tuning for Direct-Gradient Methods

images.caption: Figure 5:Comparison of GenEval score improvement during fine-tuning among ReFL, DRaFT-LV, DRTune, andLeapAlign.

Figure 6:Analysis of the nested gradient.We fine-tune the first step of the leap trajectory using only the nested gradient and varyα\alpha, which scales its magnitude. Left: average HPSv2.1 score on the test set. Right: average gradient norm during fine-tuning. Directly using the full nested gradient (α=1\alpha=1) increases the gradient norm and hurts performance, while moderate gradient discounting factor (α=0.3\alpha=0.3) provides the best trade-off.
